sentence_jp: 友達と一緒に日本語を勉強しています。
sentence_en: I'm studying Japanese together with a friend.
tip: 友 (tomo) means companion, 達 (tachi) is a pluralising suffix for people. Used for both singular and plural in practice.

sentence_jp: あの映画は本当にすごかった！
sentence_en: That movie was truly incredible!
tip: すごい alone works as 'Wow!' or 'That's amazing!' — one of the most versatile casual exclamations you'll hear constantly.

sentence_jp: 日本語の勉強は大変だけど、楽しい。
sentence_en: Studying Japanese is tough, but it's fun.
tip: 楽しむ (tanoshimu) is the verb form — 楽しんでください means 'Please enjoy yourself!' Great pair to learn together.

sentence_jp: 朝ごはんを食べましたか？
sentence_en: Did you eat breakfast?
tip: The kanji 食 appears in dozens of food words: 食事 (meal), 食堂 (cafeteria), 食料品 (groceries). Learn it early.

sentence_jp: 今日はいい天気ですね。
sentence_en: It's nice weather today, isn't it?
tip: 天気 (heavenly air) is the go-to conversation opener. 天気予報 = weather forecast. いい天気ですね is one of the first sentences every learner needs.

sentence_jp: 彼女の笑顔を見ると元気が出ます。
sentence_en: Seeing her smile gives me energy.
tip: 笑 (laugh) + 顔 (face). A 笑顔 isn't just a smile — it's the whole expression of happiness. Complimenting someone's 笑顔 is a warm, genuine thing to do.

sentence_jp: 七転び八起き！失敗しても諦めないで。
sentence_en: Fall seven times, rise eight — don't give up even when you fail.
tip: Japan's most famous resilience proverb. The spirit behind the Daruma doll — knocked over, it always rights itself. Perfect for language learning motivation.

sentence_jp: 毎日少しずつ練習することが大切です。
sentence_en: It's important to practise a little every day.
tip: 毎 (every) combines with other time words: 毎週 (every week), 毎月 (every month), 毎年 (every year). A kanji worth learning early.

sentence_jp: プレゼントをもらって、とても嬉しかったです。
sentence_en: I was so happy to receive a present.
tip: 嬉しい describes happiness at a specific event — different from 幸せ (shiawase), which is a deeper, lasting sense of happiness or contentment.

sentence_jp: いつか日本に旅行したいです。
sentence_en: I want to travel to Japan someday.
tip: 旅行する means to travel. 旅 (tabi) on its own means journey and has a more poetic, literary feel — you'll see it in song titles and poetry.

sentence_jp: 日本語が大好きです！
sentence_en: I love Japanese!
tip: 大 (big/great) intensifies 好き (like). 大好き is strong affection — for people, hobbies, or food. Saying 大好きだよ to someone is a clear declaration of love.

sentence_jp: 最近、仕事が忙しくて大変です。
sentence_en: Work has been busy lately and it's tough.
tip: A very common excuse and explanation in Japanese. 忙しそうですね (you look busy) is a polite way to acknowledge someone before bothering them.

en: To be troubled / To be at a loss
sentence_jp: お金をなくして困っています。
sentence_en: I've lost my money and I'm in trouble.
tip: 困っています (I'm in trouble/struggling) is a key phrase. 困った！ as an exclamation means 'Oh no!' or 'What a problem!' — you'll hear it a lot.

sentence_jp: 一期一会の精神で、毎日を大切に生きましょう。
sentence_en: Let's live each day preciously with the spirit of ichigo ichie.
tip: From the tea ceremony — every gathering is unique and will never happen the same way twice. One of Japan's most beautiful philosophical concepts.

sentence_jp: 電車で駅まで行きます。
sentence_en: I'll go to the station by train.
tip: 電 (electricity) + 車 (vehicle). Japan's trains are famously punctual — a delay of even a minute may come with a formal apology over the loudspeaker.

sentence_jp: 少し待ってください。
sentence_en: Please wait a moment.
tip: ちょっと待って！ (chotto matte!) is 'Wait a sec!' in casual speech. 待っていてください is the more polite 'Please wait for me'.

sentence_jp: 音楽を聴きながら勉強するのが好きです。
sentence_en: I like studying while listening to music.
tip: 音 (sound) + 楽 (enjoyment/ease). 楽器 (gakki) means musical instrument — same 楽 kanji. Japanese pop music (J-POP) is a great immersion tool.

en: To memorise / To remember / To learn
sentence_jp: 新しい単語を覚えるのが楽しい。
sentence_en: It's fun memorising new vocabulary.
tip: Don't confuse 覚える (to commit to memory) with 思い出す (omoidasu — to recall something already known). You 覚える a new word; you 思い出す a forgotten name.

sentence_jp: 夢を持つことはとても大切です。
sentence_en: Having a dream is very important.
tip: 夢 covers both sleeping dreams and life goals — unlike English, which splits 'dream' and 'goal'. 夢を叶える (yume wo kanaeru) means to make a dream come true.

sentence_jp: その映画の結末は悲しかった。
sentence_en: The ending of that film was sad.
tip: 悲しむ (kanashimu) is the verb form. 悲しみ (kanashimi) is the noun 'sadness' or 'grief'. These three — adjective, verb, noun — are worth learning as a set.

reading: せんりのみちもいっぽから
romaji: senri no michi mo ippo kara
en: Even a thousand-mile journey begins with a single step
sentence_jp: 千里の道も一歩から。今日から始めましょう！
sentence_en: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step. Let's start today!
tip: The Japanese equivalent of the famous Lao Tzu saying. 千里 is an old unit of distance — enormous. The lesson: overwhelming journeys become manageable one step at a time.

sentence_jp: 心から感謝しています。
sentence_en: I'm grateful from the bottom of my heart.
tip: 心 covers what English splits into 'heart' and 'mind'. 心から (from the heart) expresses deep sincerity. The kanji alone is one of the most evocative in the language.

sentence_jp: メールに返事をするのを忘れていました。
sentence_en: I forgot to reply to the email.
tip: 返事する means to reply. 返事がない (henji ga nai) — 'no reply' — is a phrase you'll encounter often. In Japanese culture, slow replies are noticed.

sentence_jp: 先生はとても優しくて、分かりやすく教えてくれます。
sentence_en: The teacher is very kind and explains things clearly.
tip: Same pronunciation as 易しい (yasashii — easy/simple) but different kanji. Context tells you which: 優しい人 = kind person; 易しい問題 = easy question.

sentence_jp: 日本語の文法は不思議で面白い。
sentence_en: Japanese grammar is mysterious and interesting.
tip: 不思議 carries a sense of wonder, not just strangeness. 不思議だな can mean 'how curious' or 'how wonderful' depending on tone. A great word for appreciating the unexplained.

sentence_jp: 朝、会ったら必ず挨拶をしましょう。
sentence_en: Let's always greet each other when we meet in the morning.
tip: 挨拶する means to greet. In Japanese culture, proper greetings are taken seriously — skipping them (especially with seniors) can cause real offence.

en: More haste, less speed / If you're in a hurry, take the long way round
sentence_jp: 急がば回れというように、急いでミスをするより丁寧にやった方がいい。
sentence_en: As the saying goes — if you rush and make mistakes, better to take it slow and do it right.
tip: Literally 'if you hurry, go around'. Taking the safer, longer route is faster than rushing and failing. A useful reminder in language learning too.

sentence_jp: 毎日日本語を勉強しています。
sentence_en: I study Japanese every day.
tip: 勉 means diligence and 強 means strong — the idea is that hard study makes you mentally strong. 勉強になりました (I've learned something) is a polite way to thank someone for teaching you.

sentence_jp: 大丈夫ですか？転んでしまいましたね。
sentence_en: Are you okay? You took a fall there.
tip: 大丈夫 is incredibly versatile — it can mean 'I'm fine', 'it's okay', 'no need', or even a polite refusal ('no thank you'). Context is everything.

sentence_jp: 今日はとても気持ちのいい天気ですね。
sentence_en: The weather feels really nice today, doesn't it.
tip: 気 (spirit/energy) + 持ち (holding) = the feeling you're carrying inside. 気持ちいい means something feels good — physically or emotionally.

sentence_jp: この辺では雪が降るのは珍しいです。
sentence_en: It's rare for it to snow around here.
tip: 珍 (rare/curious) is the key kanji. 珍しいですね expresses pleasant surprise. Also used for unusual food — 珍しい料理 means an unusual or exotic dish.

sentence_jp: 日本語で話せるようになりたいです。
sentence_en: I want to be able to speak in Japanese.
tip: 話す (to speak/talk) vs 言う (iu — to say) vs 伝える (tsutaeru — to convey). 話す implies a back-and-forth conversation; 言う is more about stating something.

en: To listen / To hear / To ask
sentence_jp: 音楽を聴きながら、日本語の発音を練習しています。
sentence_en: I'm practising Japanese pronunciation while listening to music.
tip: 聞く has three meanings: to listen, to hear, and to ask. When writing carefully, 聴く (with the 'ear' radical enlarged) specifically means to listen attentively.

reading: けいぞくはちからなり
en: Persistence is power / Continuity is strength
sentence_jp: 継続は力なり。毎日少しずつでも続けることが大切です。
sentence_en: Persistence is power. It's important to keep going, even a little each day.
tip: 継続 (continuation) + 力 (power/strength). The verb 継続する means to continue or sustain. This is the philosophy behind daily practice — small consistent efforts compound.

en: To do one's best / To persevere
sentence_jp: 試験に向けて頑張ります！
sentence_en: I'll do my best for the exam!
tip: Telling someone 頑張って！ is powerful encouragement — 'give it everything you've got'. 頑張れ！ is the more intense command form, shouted at sports events.

sentence_jp: この曲を聴くと、子供の頃を思い出す。懐かしいな。
sentence_en: Hearing this song makes me remember childhood. So nostalgic.
tip: 懐かしい describes the warm, bittersweet feeling of nostalgia. English has no single word that captures it as precisely.

en: I see / That makes sense / Indeed
sentence_jp: なるほど、だからそうなったんですね。
sentence_en: I see, so that's why it turned out that way.
tip: Shows you've genuinely understood something. Natural in conversation, but use carefully with superiors — it can sound slightly condescending in formal settings.

sentence_jp: このカフェ、雰囲気がいいですね。
sentence_en: This café has a great atmosphere.
tip: You'll hear 雰囲気 constantly once you know it. Japanese people are very attuned to the atmosphere of a place or situation — and 雰囲気がいい is a high compliment.

en: The nail that sticks out gets hammered down
sentence_jp: 出る杭は打たれると言うが、それでも自分らしくいたい。
sentence_en: They say the nail that sticks out gets hammered down, but I still want to be myself.
tip: Japan's most discussed cultural proverb. Describes social pressure towards conformity. Often quoted self-critically — modern Japan debates this one constantly.

en: It can't be helped / Nothing to be done
sentence_jp: 雨が降ってきたけど、仕方がない。
sentence_en: It started raining, but it can't be helped.
tip: Reflects a very Japanese acceptance of things outside one's control. Also heard as しょうがない. It's a philosophy as much as a phrase — don't fight what you can't change.

en: Good work / Thanks for your efforts
sentence_jp: お疲れ様でした！今日もよく頑張りましたね。
sentence_en: Good work today! You really gave it your all.
tip: Said at the end of work or after a hard task. It acknowledges effort — there's no real English equivalent. Otsukaresama is one of the most important workplace phrases.

sentence_jp: 彼女はいつも丁寧な言葉を使います。
sentence_en: She always uses polite language.
tip: 丁寧語 (teinei-go) is the polite speech register — the です/ます form you learn first. Being 丁寧 is a genuine compliment in Japanese culture.

sentence_jp: 洗い物をするのが面倒くさい。
sentence_en: Doing the dishes is such a bother.
tip: Extremely common in casual speech. Often shortened to めんどい by younger speakers. If someone says this about studying Japanese — tell them 頑張って！

reading: いしのうえにもさんねん
romaji: ishi no ue ni mo sannen
en: Three years even on a cold stone / Perseverance pays off
sentence_jp: 石の上にも三年。続ければ、きっと上達します。
sentence_en: Sit long enough on a cold stone — keep going and you'll definitely improve.
tip: Sit long enough on a cold stone and it warms up. Stick with something long enough and results follow. A perfect proverb for language learning.

sentence_jp: その言葉の意味を知らない。
sentence_en: I don't know the meaning of that word.
tip: 知る (shiru) means 'to come to know', so 知らない literally means 'I haven't come to know it'. Different from わからない (I don't understand) — both useful but distinct.

sentence_jp: 今日は暇だから、一緒に映画でも見ない？
sentence_en: I'm free today — want to watch a movie or something together?
tip: 暇 can mean having free time (positive) or feeling bored/idle (slightly negative). 暇人 (hima-jin) is a person with nothing to do — can be affectionate or dismissive depending on tone.

en: As expected / Just as I thought / After all
sentence_jp: やっぱり、日本語は難しいですね。
sentence_en: As I expected, Japanese is difficult.
tip: Also やはり in formal speech. One of the most satisfying words — it confirms your instinct was right all along. Listen for it constantly in natural conversation.

reading: わらうかどにはふくきたる
romaji: warau kado ni wa fuku kitaru
en: Fortune comes to those who smile / Laughter brings happiness
sentence_jp: 笑う門には福来るというから、いつも笑顔でいましょう。
sentence_en: As they say, fortune comes to those who smile — let's always keep a smile.
tip: 門 (kado) means gate or household. The image is of fortune arriving at the doorstep of a cheerful home. One of Japan's most optimistic proverbs.

sentence_jp: 彼は素直な性格で、みんなに好かれています。
sentence_en: He has a genuine, straightforward personality and is liked by everyone.
tip: 素直 describes someone who doesn't put up a front — genuine, easy to deal with, and open to guidance. A deeply valued trait in Japan.

sentence_jp: 本音を言えば、本当は行きたくなかった。
sentence_en: If I'm being honest, I didn't really want to go.
tip: 本音 (true sound) is contrasted with 建前 (tatemae — public face). This distinction is central to Japanese social culture: what someone says publicly vs. what they truly feel.

sentence_jp: 新しい単語はすぐに忘れてしまいます。
sentence_en: I forget new vocabulary so quickly.
tip: 忘れ物 (wasuremono) means a forgotten item or lost property — you'll see this sign at train stations. 忘れないで！ = Don't forget!

sentence_jp: 毎日少しずつ練習すれば、必ず上達します。
sentence_en: If you practise a little every day, you'll definitely improve.
tip: 練習する = to practise. 反復練習 (hanpuku renshuu) is repetition drilling. Spaced repetition is just 間隔を置いた練習 — the same concept, Japanese style.

sentence_jp: 知らぬが仏というけど、知った方がいいこともある。
sentence_en: They say ignorance is bliss, but sometimes it's better to know.
tip: Literally 'not knowing is Buddha' — the ignorant person is at peace, like a serene Buddha who doesn't know what they're missing. The Japanese counterpart to 'ignorance is bliss'.

en: To read the room / To pick up on the atmosphere
sentence_jp: 空気が読めない人とは一緒にいるのが難しい。
sentence_en: It's hard to be around people who can't read the room.
tip: 空気 means 'air/atmosphere' and 読む means 'to read'. Reading the air — picking up on unspoken social cues — is considered an essential social skill in Japan.

en: To give up / To abandon / To resign oneself
sentence_jp: 夢を諦めないでください。
sentence_en: Please don't give up on your dreams.
tip: Comes from Buddhist philosophy — 諦 relates to accepting truth. In older usage it meant 'to see clearly', not just 'to quit'. 諦めが悪い = stubborn (won't give up).

sentence_jp: 日本語で話すとき、相槌を打つことが大切です。
sentence_en: When speaking Japanese, it's important to make listening sounds.
tip: Japanese conversation depends on frequent あいづち — はい, そうですね, なるほど, ええ. Staying silent while someone talks can seem rude or inattentive.

sentence_jp: 今日、初めて寿司を食べました！
sentence_en: Today I ate sushi for the first time!
tip: Don't confuse 初めて (hajimete — first time) with 始める (hajimeru — to start/begin). Same reading family, different kanji and meaning.

reading: しょしんわするべからず
sentence_jp: 初心忘るべからず。上手くなっても、最初の気持ちを大切に。
sentence_en: Never forget the beginner's spirit. Even as you improve, treasure that first feeling.
tip: 初心 (shoshin) = beginner's mind. べからず is classical Japanese for 'must not'. A reminder that humility and fresh eyes are assets at any level of skill.

sentence_jp: もう少しだけ我慢してください。
sentence_en: Please hold on just a little longer.
tip: 我慢 isn't just patience — it implies stoically bearing hardship. A deeply respected quality in Japanese culture, particularly in adversity.

sentence_jp: 言葉は文化を映す鏡です。
sentence_en: Language is a mirror that reflects culture.
tip: 言葉 can mean a single word, language as a whole, or the things someone says. 言葉を選ぶ (choose one's words carefully) is a phrase worth knowing early.

sentence_jp: 縁があれば、また会えるでしょう。
sentence_en: If fate allows, we'll meet again.
tip: 縁 refers to the mysterious thread that connects people — the idea that meetings aren't random but fated. A deeply Buddhist concept woven into everyday Japanese.

en: Birds of a feather flock together
sentence_jp: 類は友を呼ぶというか、同じ趣味の人同士が集まっていますね。
sentence_en: True to 'birds of a feather' — people with the same interests tend to gather together.
tip: 類 means 'kind/type', so 同じ類 = the same kind. The proverb observes that similar people naturally attract each other — useful for explaining friend groups.

en: Let's eat / Thank you for this meal
sentence_jp: では、いただきます！
sentence_en: Well then, let's eat!
tip: Said before every meal. A humble expression of receiving — thanking the food, the cook, the farmers, and nature. There's no English translation that covers it all.

en: Thank you for the meal (after eating)
sentence_jp: ごちそうさまでした！とても美味しかったです。
sentence_en: Thank you for the meal! It was delicious.
tip: The pair to いただきます. 馳走 (chisou) originally meant 'running around' — referring to the effort of gathering ingredients. A meal is an act of care.

sentence_jp: この道をまっすぐ行くと、駅に着きます。
sentence_en: If you go straight down this road, you'll reach the station.
tip: 道 has a philosophical dimension too — 武道 (budo/martial way), 茶道 (chado/tea way), 書道 (shodo/calligraphy way). The path of mastery is 道.

en: You reap what you sow / Troubles of your own making
sentence_jp: 身から出た錆だから、しっかり責任を取らなければならない。
sentence_en: It's trouble of your own making — you have to take proper responsibility.
tip: Literally 'rust that came from one's own body'. A sword rusts from its own iron — your troubles come from within you. A stark, honest proverb about taking responsibility.

sentence_jp: 公園を歩きながら木漏れ日を楽しんだ。
sentence_en: I enjoyed the dappled sunlight while walking through the park.
tip: One of Japanese's most beautiful untranslatable words. The interplay of light and shadow through tree leaves. English needs a whole phrase; Japanese has one elegant word.

sentence_jp: 秋の夕暮れは物悲しい気持ちになる。
sentence_en: Autumn dusk fills me with a melancholy feeling.
tip: 物 (mono) here means 'things in general' — a vague sadness that arises from the nature of things. Related to 物の哀れ (mono no aware), a key concept in Japanese aesthetics.

sentence_jp: 古い茶碗の傷にも侘び寂びの美しさがある。
sentence_en: There is a wabi-sabi beauty even in the cracks of an old tea bowl.
tip: Perhaps the most famous Japanese aesthetic concept. 侘び (wabi) = simplicity and asymmetry; 寂び (sabi) = beauty that comes with age and wear. Imperfection is the point.

jp: 案ずるより産むが易し
reading: あんずるよりうむがやすし
en: It's easier to do than to worry about doing
sentence_jp: 案ずるより産むが易しというから、まずやってみよう。
sentence_en: As they say, it's easier done than feared — let's just try it.
tip: Literally 'giving birth is easier than worrying about it'. The fear of starting is often worse than the task itself. Perfect encouragement for trying to speak Japanese.

sentence_jp: 料理が私の生きがいです。
sentence_en: Cooking is my reason for living.
tip: 生き (iki) = life, 甲斐 (gai) = worth/value. Your ikigai is what gets you out of bed in the morning. Now a global concept — but it started here.

sentence_jp: 週末は山で森林浴をして、ストレスを解消した。
sentence_en: I relieved stress on the weekend with some forest bathing in the mountains.
tip: A recognised health practice in Japan — spending time in forests to reduce cortisol and improve wellbeing. 浴 (yoku) means 'bath' — you bathe in the atmosphere of the forest.

en: Restraint / Holding back out of politeness
sentence_jp: どうぞ遠慮なく食べてください。
sentence_en: Please help yourself — don't hold back.
tip: 遠慮 is the polite act of restraining yourself so as not to impose. When a host says 遠慮しないで, they're genuinely urging you to stop being polite and dig in.

en: A piece of cake / Easy as pie
sentence_jp: そんな計算、朝飯前だよ。
sentence_en: That calculation? Easy — before-breakfast stuff.
tip: Literally 'before morning rice' — something so simple you could do it before breakfast without effort. The Japanese equivalent of 'a piece of cake'.

en: To sense / To infer / To read between the lines
sentence_jp: 彼女の気持ちを察して、そっとしておいた。
sentence_en: I sensed how she was feeling and left her in peace.
tip: 察する is the ability to understand what someone feels without being told. Highly valued in Japan — it's the skill underlying 空気を読む (reading the room).

sentence_jp: 語学の上達は毎日の積み重ねが大切です。
sentence_en: For language improvement, daily accumulation is what matters.
tip: 積み重ね literally means 'stack of piles' — the image of progress building layer by layer. A word that applies perfectly to language learning.

reading: あめふってじかたまる
en: After rain, the ground hardens / Adversity strengthens
sentence_jp: 雨降って地固まるというように、あの困難を乗り越えてチームはもっと強くなった。
sentence_en: As they say, after the rain the ground hardens — overcoming that difficulty made the team stronger.
tip: Rain makes the earth soft, but once it dries, the ground is firmer than before. Hardship, when overcome, leaves you stronger. A powerful and comforting proverb.

sentence_jp: 会話の間が大切なこともある。
sentence_en: The pauses in a conversation can matter just as much.
tip: 間 is a foundational Japanese aesthetic concept — the meaningful pause, the space between things, the rest in music. Silence in Japan communicates as much as speech.

sentence_jp: 子供の頃の思い出は宝物です。
sentence_en: Memories from childhood are treasures.
tip: 思い出 has a warm, nostalgic feel — it's almost always good memories. 思い出す (omoidasu) means to recall or remember something.

sentence_jp: 好きな科目を自由に選べます。
sentence_en: You can freely choose your favourite subject.
tip: 選ぶ vs 決める (kimeru — to decide): 選ぶ emphasises choosing from options; 決める emphasises making a firm decision. Related noun: 選択 (sentaku) = choice/selection.

en: Losing is winning / Sometimes it pays to concede
sentence_jp: 負けるが勝ちという言葉の通り、今回は引いた方が賢明だ。
sentence_en: As the saying goes, losing is winning — stepping back this time is the wiser move.
tip: Conceding a small battle to win the war. Refusing to fight an unwinnable argument can be the smartest move. A very Japanese approach to conflict — harmony over pride.

sentence_jp: 毎朝日本語を読む習慣をつけました。
sentence_en: I've built a habit of reading Japanese every morning.
tip: 習慣 = established habit. 習慣化する means to make something a habit. Language learning lives or dies on 習慣 — good habits compound over time.

en: Good at / Strong point / Skilled in
sentence_jp: 読むのは得意ですが、話すのはまだ苦手です。
sentence_en: I'm good at reading, but speaking is still a weak point.
tip: Often paired with its opposite 苦手 (nigate — weak point/not good at). 得意になる = to become good at something. Also means 'proud' in some contexts.

en: Not good at / Weak point / Something disliked
sentence_jp: カタカナが苦手ですが、練習しています。
sentence_en: I'm not good at katakana, but I'm practising.
tip: 苦手 (bitter + skilled) — the 'bitter' feeling of struggling with something. Can be used for skills or people: あの人が苦手 = 'I find that person difficult to deal with'.

sentence_jp: 長年の友達だから、以心伝心で気持ちが伝わる。
sentence_en: We've been friends for so long that we understand each other without words.
tip: Four-character idiom: 以 (by means of) + 心 (heart) + 伝 (transmit) + 心 (heart). The idea that deep understanding can be communicated without speech — essential in Japanese culture.

sentence_jp: 間違えてしまって恥ずかしかった。
sentence_en: I made a mistake and felt embarrassed.
tip: 恥ずかしい covers both embarrassment and shyness. 恥 (haji) alone is a stronger word — 'shame' or 'disgrace'. 恥ずかしがり屋 = a shy person.

sentence_jp: それは当たり前のことですよ。
sentence_en: That's just a natural thing to do.
tip: 当たり前 describes something so obvious it goes without saying. 当たり前でしょ！ = 'Of course!/Obviously!' Used both sincerely and sarcastically.

sentence_jp: 家族はとても大切な存在です。
sentence_en: Family is a very precious presence in one's life.
tip: 大切 (important/precious) vs 重要 (juuyou — important/significant). 大切 has emotional weight — things and people you treasure. 重要 is more formal/abstract.

en: Kill two birds with one stone
sentence_jp: 運動しながら通勤するなんて一石二鳥ですね。
sentence_en: Exercising during your commute — that's killing two birds with one stone.
tip: A four-character idiom with the same image as the English expression. 一石 = one stone, 二鳥 = two birds. Borrowed the concept, kept the elegance.

jp: よろしくお願いします
reading: よろしくおねがいします
en: Please treat me well / Nice to meet you / I'm counting on you
sentence_jp: はじめまして、田中です。よろしくお願いします。
sentence_en: Nice to meet you, I'm Tanaka. I look forward to working with you.
tip: One of the most important and hardest-to-translate phrases in Japanese. Said when meeting someone, starting a project, or asking a favour. The nuance is 'please be kind to me going forward'.

en: To be surprised / To be astonished
sentence_jp: 彼の日本語力に驚きました。
sentence_en: I was astonished at his Japanese ability.
tip: 驚き (odoroki) is the noun — surprise, astonishment. びっくりする is the more casual equivalent: びっくりした！= 'I was shocked!' 驚く sounds slightly more refined.

sentence_jp: 日本語仲間を見つけると、勉強が楽しくなります。
sentence_en: Finding Japanese study companions makes learning much more enjoyable.
tip: 仲間 carries a strong sense of camaraderie — not just friends, but people united by a shared purpose or group. Famous for its use in anime to describe the bonds of a team.

jp: 二兎を追う者は一兎をも得ず
reading: にとをおうものはいっとをもえず
romaji: nito wo ou mono wa itto wo mo ezu
en: He who chases two rabbits catches neither
sentence_jp: 二兎を追う者は一兎をも得ず。一つのことに集中しよう。
sentence_en: He who chases two rabbits catches neither — let's focus on one thing.
tip: A warning against trying to do too many things at once. 兎 (to/usagi) = rabbit. The image is vivid — split your attention and both targets escape.

sentence_jp: 最近、日本語が上達してきた気がします。
sentence_en: I feel like my Japanese has been improving lately.
tip: 上達する = to improve/get better at a skill. 上達が早い = a fast learner. This is the word to use when your language study is paying off — celebrate your 上達!

en: To be curious about / To be on one's mind / To bother
sentence_jp: あの店が気になって、毎日前を通っています。
sentence_en: That shop has been on my mind — I've been walking past it every day.
tip: 気になる can mean you're curious, intrigued, or slightly bothered. 気になる人 can mean someone you have a crush on. 気にする = to worry/care about something.

sentence_jp: 小さなことに幸せを感じることができます。
sentence_en: You can find happiness in small things.
tip: 幸せ is deep, lasting happiness — distinct from 嬉しい (ureshii — happiness at a specific event). 幸せになってほしい = I want you to be happy. A word of sincere weight.

reading: りょうやくはくちににがし
en: Good medicine tastes bitter / Honest advice is hard to hear
sentence_jp: 良薬は口に苦しというように、厳しいアドバイスが一番役に立つことがある。
sentence_en: Good medicine tastes bitter — sometimes the harshest advice is the most useful.
tip: Medicine that cures you tastes bad; advice that helps you may sting. One of Japan's most practical proverbs — about accepting difficult truths from people who care.

sentence_jp: スピーチの前はいつも緊張します。
sentence_en: I always get nervous before giving a speech.
tip: 緊張する = to be nervous/tense. 緊張しないで！= Don't be nervous! The opposite is リラックスする or 落ち着く (ochitsuku — to calm down).

en: To calm down / To settle / To feel at ease
sentence_jp: 深呼吸して、落ち着いてください。
sentence_en: Take a deep breath and calm down.
tip: 落ち着いた人 = a calm, composed person. 落ち着いた雰囲気 = a relaxed atmosphere. The ついた form (having calmed down) is very common: 落ち着いた？= Are you calmer now?

sentence_jp: 音楽を止めた方が集中できます。
sentence_en: I can concentrate better with the music off.
tip: 集中する = to focus/concentrate. 集中力 (shuuchuuryoku) = concentration ability. 集中できない！= I can't focus! — familiar for anyone trying to study.

reading: そなえあればうれいなし
sentence_jp: 備えあれば憂いなし。試験の前はしっかり準備しておこう。
sentence_en: Prepare and have no worries — let's study properly before the exam.
tip: 備え (sonae) = preparation, 憂い (urei) = worry/sorrow. If you are prepared, you have nothing to fear. A proverb that rewards action over anxiety.

sentence_jp: 自由に日本語で話せるようになりたいです。
sentence_en: I want to be able to speak freely in Japanese.
tip: 自由 (self + reason/cause) — freedom arising from your own will. 自由に = freely, without restriction. 自由時間 = free time. The word feels expansive, like opening a window.

en: To change / To vary / To be different
sentence_jp: 日本語を勉強し始めてから、視野が変わりました。
sentence_en: Since I started studying Japanese, my perspective has changed.
tip: 変わる (intransitive — it changes) vs 変える (kaeru — transitive — you change it). This distinction (intransitive/transitive verb pairs) is a key feature of Japanese grammar.

sentence_jp: 諦めずに続けることが大切です。
sentence_en: It's important to keep going without giving up.
tip: 続ける attaches to other verbs: 勉強し続ける = to keep studying, 話し続ける = to keep talking. 続く (tsuzuku) is the intransitive version — 'it continues'.

reading: えんのしたのちからもち
en: The unsung hero / One who supports from behind the scenes
sentence_jp: 彼はいつも縁の下の力持ちで、みんなを支えています。
sentence_en: He's always the unsung hero, supporting everyone from behind the scenes.
tip: Literally 'the strong person under the floor'. Traditional Japanese houses had a crawl space — someone doing heavy lifting there would never be seen. Beautiful image for invisible support.

en: To understand / To know / To be clear
sentence_jp: この文法がまだよく分かりません。
sentence_en: I still don't quite understand this grammar.
tip: 分かる is 'to understand' rather than 'to know a fact' (知る). 分かりました = I understand (past polite). 分かりました！ after an explanation is the polite way to confirm understanding.

sentence_jp: 彼女は真面目な学生で、毎日欠かさず勉強します。
sentence_en: She's a diligent student who studies every day without fail.
tip: 真面目 is a high compliment in Japanese culture — it describes someone dependable, earnest, and hard-working. まじか！(majika!) is a completely different casual expression meaning 'Seriously?!'

en: Sometimes a white lie is necessary
sentence_jp: 嘘も方便というけど、相手を傷つけないためなら仕方がない。
sentence_en: As they say, sometimes a white lie is necessary — especially if it saves someone from being hurt.
tip: 方便 (houben) is a Buddhist term for a skilful means of teaching — sometimes a simpler, indirect truth serves better. Gives philosophical weight to the social necessity of tact.

en: To be careful / To watch out / To take care
sentence_jp: 道を渡るときは、気をつけてください。
sentence_en: Please be careful when crossing the road.
tip: 気をつけて！ = Take care! (Said when someone leaves.) 気をつけてください = Please be careful. One of the most frequently used everyday expressions.

sentence_jp: どんなに上手くなっても謙虚でいることが大切です。
sentence_en: No matter how skilled you become, it's important to stay humble.
tip: Humility is a core value in Japanese culture. 謙虚な人 is someone you'd want on your team. The opposite, 傲慢 (gouman), means arrogant — a serious criticism.

sentence_jp: この曲を聴くと、何となく切ない気持ちになる。
sentence_en: Listening to this song somehow fills me with a bittersweet feeling.
tip: 切ない is a beautiful emotional word — the ache of something slipping away, a sweet sadness. Common in song lyrics and poetry. Very hard to translate perfectly into English.

en: Dumplings over flowers / Practical pleasures over aesthetic ones
sentence_jp: 花見より花より団子で、食べ物目当てで来ました。
sentence_en: It's dumplings over flowers for me — I came for the food.
tip: Said of someone who prefers practical pleasures (food, comfort) over aesthetic ones (viewing cherry blossoms). The source of the famous manga title 花より男子.

sentence_jp: 日本旅行のことを考えるとわくわくします！
sentence_en: Just thinking about the trip to Japan gets me excited!
tip: An onomatopoeic word (擬態語) imitating the flutter of excitement. わくわくする = to feel excited. どきどきする (heartbeat racing) is similar but leans more towards nervous excitement.

sentence_jp: 彼のことを考えると、どきどきしてしまいます。
sentence_en: Just thinking about him makes my heart race.
tip: An onomatopoeic word for a pounding heart. どきどきする = heart beating fast — from excitement, fear, or love. Contrast with わくわく (excited/eager) and ドキッとする (sudden startle).

sentence_jp: 友達が引っ越してしまって、寂しいです。
sentence_en: My friend moved away and I feel lonely.
tip: Also read as さみしい (samishii) — both are correct, with さみしい feeling slightly softer. 寂しさ = loneliness (noun). A deeply human word that appears often in Japanese poetry and song.

en: To wear a cat / To hide one's true nature
sentence_jp: 彼女は先生の前では猫をかぶっています。
sentence_en: She wears a cat (acts sweet and innocent) in front of the teacher.
tip: Literally 'to wear a cat' — pretending to be tame and harmless when you're actually not. The Japanese equivalent of 'wolf in sheep's clothing', but cuter.

en: Frustrating / Vexing / Feeling of regret at a loss
sentence_jp: もう少しで合格だったのに、悔しい！
sentence_en: I was so close to passing — it's so frustrating!
tip: 悔しい is the bitter frustration of narrowly missing something or being defeated unfairly. Stronger than just 'frustrated' — it carries wounded pride and determination to do better.

en: Polite speech / Polite register (です・ます form)
sentence_jp: 初対面の人には丁寧語を使うのがマナーです。
sentence_en: It's good manners to use polite speech with someone you're meeting for the first time.
tip: Japanese has distinct speech registers. 丁寧語 (です/ます) is the polite form most learners start with. Above it: 尊敬語 (sonkei-go, respectful) and 謙譲語 (kenjou-go, humble).

sentence_jp: 頭がいい人は、物事をシンプルに考えます。
sentence_en: Smart people think about things simply.
tip: 頭 means the physical head and also intellect. 頭がいい = smart/intelligent. 頭が痛い = headache (also used figuratively for 'this is a headache of a problem').

sentence_jp: 手を洗ってから、ご飯を食べましょう。
sentence_en: Let's wash our hands before eating.
tip: 手 (te) appears in dozens of compounds: 上手 (jouzu — skilled), 下手 (heta — unskilled), 握手 (akushu — handshake). The most useful body-part kanji.

sentence_jp: 春になると、公園に色とりどりの花が咲きます。
sentence_en: When spring comes, flowers of all colours bloom in the park.
tip: 花 appears in 花火 (hanabi — fireworks, literally 'flower fire'), 花見 (hanami — flower viewing), 花束 (hanataba — bouquet). Central to Japanese aesthetics and culture.

sentence_jp: 正直に言うと、その映画はあまり面白くなかった。
sentence_en: Honestly speaking, that movie wasn't very interesting.
tip: 正直に言うと = honestly speaking (useful phrase). 正直者 = an honest person. 正直は最善の策 is the Japanese way of saying 'honesty is the best policy'.

sentence_jp: ビジネスの場では敬語を使うことが重要です。
sentence_en: Using honorific language is important in business settings.
tip: 敬語 is the formal speech system: 尊敬語 (for lifting up others), 謙譲語 (for lowering yourself), 丁寧語 (general politeness). Even native Japanese speakers find keigo tricky.

reading: さわらぬかみにたたりなし
sentence_jp: 触らぬ神に祟りなし。あの件には関わらない方がいいよ。
sentence_en: Let sleeping dogs lie — better not to get involved in that matter.
tip: Literally 'if you don't touch the god, there's no curse'. Don't poke dangerous things unnecessarily. 祟り (tatari) means divine curse or misfortune. A very practical proverb.

sentence_jp: 合格の知らせを聞いて喜びで涙が出た。
sentence_en: Hearing the news of passing, tears of joy came to my eyes.
tip: 喜ぶ (yorokobu) is the verb — to rejoice, to be pleased. 喜んで！= With pleasure!/Gladly! (A natural way to accept a request or invitation.)

sentence_jp: 日本語を学ぶと、日本の文化も自然と理解できるようになります。
sentence_en: When you learn Japanese, you naturally come to understand Japanese culture too.
tip: 文 (writing/culture) + 化 (transformation). 文化の日 is Culture Day in Japan (November 3rd). Learning a language is inseparable from learning its culture.

sentence_jp: 単語の記憶を助けるために、例文を使っています。
sentence_en: I use example sentences to help memorise vocabulary.
tip: 記憶 = the faculty of memory (abstract). 思い出 (omoide) = a specific cherished memory. 記憶する = to memorise. 記憶力 = memory ability. All worth knowing.

reading: かめのこうよりとしのこう
romaji: kame no kou yori toshi no kou
en: Experience beats everything / Wisdom of age
sentence_jp: 亀の甲より年の功というから、先輩のアドバイスは大切にしよう。
sentence_en: As they say, experience beats everything — let's value our seniors' advice.
tip: 亀 (kame) = tortoise, 甲 (kou) = shell (hard/valuable thing). Even harder than a tortoise shell is the experience of years. A proverb about respecting lived wisdom.

en: To think / To consider / To reflect
sentence_jp: もう少し考えてから、答えます。
sentence_en: I'll think a little more and then give you an answer.
tip: 考える (to think/consider) vs 思う (omou — to feel/think/believe). 考える implies reasoning; 思う is more instinctive or emotional. Both are essential.

en: To decide / To determine / To settle
sentence_jp: もう決めました。日本語を絶対にマスターします！
sentence_en: I've decided. I'm absolutely going to master Japanese!
tip: 決める (transitive — you decide) vs 決まる (kimaru — intransitive, it's decided). 決めた！= It's decided!/I've made up my mind! A satisfying, definitive word.

sentence_jp: 日本語が話せると、日本での生活がより豊かになります。
sentence_en: Being able to speak Japanese enriches your life in Japan.
tip: 生活 means daily life as a practice — the lived experience of each day. 生活する = to live/get by. 日常生活 (daily life), 生活費 (living expenses) are common compounds.

jp: 三人寄れば文殊の知恵
reading: さんにんよればもんじゅのちえ
en: Two heads are better than one
sentence_jp: 三人寄れば文殊の知恵というから、みんなで考えましょう。
sentence_en: As they say, many minds are better than one — let's think together.
tip: 文殊 (Monju) is the bodhisattva of wisdom. Three people together can match the wisdom of Monju. A Buddhist-rooted proverb about the value of collective thinking.

sentence_jp: 日本には四季があり、それぞれの季節に美しさがあります。
sentence_en: Japan has four seasons, and each one has its own beauty.
tip: The four seasons — 春 (haru, spring), 夏 (natsu, summer), 秋 (aki, autumn), 冬 (fuyu, winter) — are central to Japanese culture, poetry, and food.

sentence_jp: この値段は少し高いですね。
sentence_en: This price is a little high, isn't it?
tip: 値段 = the price you see on a tag. 値 (ne) = value, 段 = level/step. Related: 高い (takai — expensive), 安い (yasui — cheap), 値引き (neびき — discount).

sentence_jp: 日本語を勉強している理由はなんですか？
sentence_en: What's your reason for studying Japanese?
tip: 理由 (reason/cause) vs 原因 (genin — cause of a problem/incident). 理由 is neutral; 原因 often implies a negative cause. 理由を説明する = to explain one's reasons.

reading: くちはわざわいのもと
en: The mouth is the source of trouble
sentence_jp: 口は禍の元というから、余計なことは言わない方がいいよ。
sentence_en: As they say, the mouth is the source of trouble — better not to say anything unnecessary.
tip: A reminder that careless speech causes harm. 禍 (wazawai) means misfortune or disaster. 言わぬが花 (iwanu ga hana — not saying is the flower) is a softer version of the same idea.

sentence_jp: 運動後は喉が渇きますね。
sentence_en: You get thirsty after exercising, don't you?
tip: 喉 (nodo) = throat. 渇く (kawaku) = to dry out/become parched. Japanese often describes physical states this way: 喉が渇いた = I'm thirsty (literally 'throat has dried').

sentence_jp: お腹が空いたので、何か食べたいです。
sentence_en: I'm hungry, so I want to eat something.
tip: お腹 (onaka) = stomach (polite). 空く (suku) = to become empty. So 'stomach has become empty' = hungry. お腹いっぱい = I'm full (stomach is full).

sentence_jp: 眠くて、目が開かない。
sentence_en: I'm so sleepy I can barely keep my eyes open.
tip: 眠い = sleepy (adjective). 眠る (nemuru) = to sleep. 眠気 (nemuke) = sleepiness. 眠れない (nemurenai) = can't sleep. A whole family of words around sleep.

sentence_jp: 花より男子は有名な漫画ですね。
sentence_en: Hana Yori Dango is a famous manga, isn't it?
tip: A pun on the proverb 花より団子 (dumplings over flowers). Swapping 団子 (dango) for 男子 (danshi — boys) creates the title. A clever wordplay example of how Japanese culture loves linguistic games.

sentence_jp: 今日は早く家に帰りたいです。
sentence_en: I want to go home early today.
tip: 帰る (kaeru) = to return/go home vs 戻る (modoru) = to return/come back. 帰る implies going to your home base; 戻る implies coming back to a place you were. お帰りなさい = 'Welcome home'.

reading: あたまかくしてしりかくさず
en: Hiding your head but not your bottom / An obvious disguise
sentence_jp: 頭隠して尻隠さず。嘘をついても、すぐバレてしまう。
sentence_en: Hiding your head but not your rear — lying doesn't work when the truth is obvious.
tip: From the image of a bird burying its head in a bush but leaving its tail sticking out. The Japanese equivalent of 'putting your head in the sand'. Perfect for describing badly concealed deceptions.

en: Long time no see / It's been a while
sentence_jp: 久しぶり！元気にしてた？
sentence_en: Long time no see! Have you been well?
tip: お久しぶりです is the polite version. 久しぶりに食べた！= I ate that for the first time in ages! 久しぶりの can attach to nouns: 久しぶりの再会 = long-awaited reunion.

sentence_jp: このカバンは丈夫で、何年も使えます。
sentence_en: This bag is sturdy and can be used for many years.
tip: Don't confuse 丈夫 (joubu — sturdy) with 大丈夫 (daijoubu — okay/fine). 丈夫な体 = a robust body. 体が丈夫 = physically strong/healthy.
